title: Is the AIADMK Trying to Deceive the People by Promising Prohibition in Its Election Manifesto?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil reading copy corrects the scan-confirmed year `1911` to `1971` and repairs unambiguous OCR joins including `நிறை வேற்றப்பட்டது`, `செல்போன்டவரில்`, `குடியமர்ந்துள் ள`, and `தோரதல்`. The source's repeated TASMAC history and election-manifesto warning are preserved. The canonical Tamil source file is unchanged.
paragraphs: On 21 July 2015, I announced: “If the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam comes to power, vigorous steps will be taken to implement prohibition.”

In a statement issued on 3 August 2015, I said: “On Monday, 10 August 2015, the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam will conduct peaceful demonstrations in district headquarters under the leadership of the respective district secretaries, demanding the implementation of prohibition.” The demonstrations were accordingly conducted successfully throughout Tamil Nadu.

Whenever this issue of prohibition is discussed, some people make a sweeping allegation with an ulterior motive: “Was it not while the DMK was in office that prohibition was repealed? It is therefore the DMK that ruined everything.” They keep repeating what they have already said for political reasons, hoping repetition will make it accepted as settled truth.

Although prohibition was postponed under the DMK government, I said even then that the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam did not take the decision wholeheartedly. I have explained many times that Tamil Nadu's financial condition at the time was the reason.

It was the AIADMK government led by Jayalalithaa that established TASMAC for liquor sales in 2003 and had the government itself open liquor shops from town to town. Yet certain people try to hide this vital fact from public view rather than state it openly without doing violence to their conscience.
What did I say even as I addressed the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly while postponing prohibition?

“Gandhiji said that this sacred policy should be extended throughout the whole land of India. Yet not only were the Chief Ministers of the states—the commanders of the army that stood arrayed beneath the shadow of the flag he bore—unable to follow that policy; it was also neglected by the Mahatma's successors who run the Central government. That is a painful truth. How long can Tamil Nadu preserve itself like unlit camphor within a ring of blazing fire?”
That is what I said in the Assembly. I announced the postponement of the prohibition law only in a situation my conscience could not accept.

Replying to the general debate on the budget statement on 29 June 1971, I said: “I respect and appreciate the feelings of the honourable members and friends from our allied parties. Some have even pleaded with me. They said they were imploring me. I am younger than every one of them. Please withdraw that pleading and imploring. We reached this decision only because there was no other way. Even then, we have said only that it has been postponed.”

Although the DMK government opened toddy and arrack shops in 1971, it was again during the DMK government in 1974 that the liquor shops were closed and prohibition restored. Those who never forget to mention the opening should consider whether it is right to conceal, for convenience, the fact that they were closed.

Does not postponement itself imply that a measure will return? It was during the DMK government that a bill implementing complete prohibition in Tamil Nadu was unanimously passed by the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly on 22 August 1974.
What did the AIADMK government that followed do? The truth—the truth—is that the DMK government itself withdrew its earlier repeal of prohibition and brought prohibition back into force.

But in 1981, Chief Minister M. G. R. once again repealed prohibition to permit the sale of toddy and arrack. Certain people do not proclaim this truth aloud. The reason is the fear that descends whenever the AIADMK is in power!

Later, when today's Chief Minister Jayalalithaa came to power in 2001, she proclaimed a campaign against liquor. Yet in that year of her government alone, cases were registered against 2 lakh and 75 thousand people under the prohibition law. When Jayalalithaa briefly stepped down because of a Supreme Court judgment and O. Panneerselvam was Chief Minister, he announced that foreign liquor varieties would be sold from the first week of January 2002 at the cheap price of Rs 15 per 100 millilitres.

It was the AIADMK government led by Jayalalithaa that established TASMAC for liquor sales in 2003 and had the government itself open liquor shops from town to town. Yet certain people try to hide this vital fact from public view rather than state it openly without doing violence to their conscience.

A few months ago, I announced that if the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam returned to office in Tamil Nadu, steps would be taken to implement the policy of prohibition. Leaders and leading figures from various parties, social activists, journalists and women's organisations contacted me by telephone and in person, welcomed the announcement and called it a good decision. It is no exaggeration to say that my announcement had an enormous impact across every section.

On 22 December 2008, when PMK founder Dr Ramadoss and leaders of various organisations met me and urged the implementation of prohibition in Tamil Nadu, I reminded them that the DMK government, which shared their view, had closed 1,300 drinking halls—bars—throughout Tamil Nadu after coming to office in 2006, and had also closed 128 retail liquor shops.

I further announced that efforts would continue toward complete prohibition in stages. Until then, the government had decided to act carefully to prevent liquor shops from being located near public places such as temples, schools, colleges, mosques and churches, and to reduce their opening hours by one hour. An order was issued accordingly.

Some people campaign as though prohibition was postponed for the first time only under the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam government. That too is untrue. When the Madras Presidency government was formed under Rajaji in 1937, he introduced prohibition, which until then had not operated in the Presidency. But even that ended in 1939. In 1937 itself, while Rajaji was Chief Minister, prohibition was in force in only four of the Presidency's twenty-five districts: Salem, Chittoor, Cuddapah and North Arcot. The conditions that existed before 1937 continued in the remaining districts. Complete prohibition was implemented in Tamil Nadu only after Omandurar became Chief Minister in 1948. At other times—both before 1937 and between 1939 and 1948—prohibition was not in force in Tamil Nadu. A little reflection makes clear how contrary to truth is the claim that the DMK government was the first to introduce liquor into Tamil Nadu, and what a distortion it is to hold the DMK responsible.

Indeed, my dear friend M. G. R. issued a statement on prohibition on 13 September 1969. At its conclusion, he wrote: “On the policy of prohibition, I can boldly and more emphatically than anyone else declare that the DMK leader and Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu, Kalaignar, has greater concern and commitment to the people's need for that policy than any individual or political party, not only in Tamil Nadu but anywhere in the Indian subcontinent.” He later wrote about it at length in his serialised essay, “Why Was I Born?”, in *Ananda Vikatan*. Those now in the AIADMK under Jayalalithaa—perhaps the newcomers—may have forgotten it. I have not. Nor have those who have continued to observe this issue closely.

Gandhian Sasiperumal had not even reached sixty. His protest was simply to demand the closure of one TASMAC shop in Kanyakumari district. Had they agreed to close that one shop, no crisis would have arisen and the protest would not have spread across Tamil Nadu. But what did this government ever attend to, that it should attend to this? How many hours could he remain on a mobile-phone tower? Had the responsible officials arrived within an hour, discussed the issue and found a solution, would Sasiperumal, who had dedicated his life to prohibition, have died?

Even after every political party in Tamil Nadu issued statements on prohibition, spontaneous protests continued and police repression was unleashed. Yet Chief Minister Jayalalithaa did not utter a single word at the time. The reason was the authoritarian attitude lodged in her mind! An *Ananda Vikatan* editorial published then under the title “Let Evil Depart” supplies an apt response:

“Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has so far said not a single word about prohibition, reducing the number of liquor shops, or the protests against TASMAC shops. Even before Sasiperumal's death, protests against TASMAC had begun across Tamil Nadu in full force. They intensified after his death. College students, homemakers and public-interest organisations continue to protest throughout Tamil Nadu. Every political party is present in the field in its own way.

“As the protests began to intensify, reports were leaked from government circles to the media that the Chief Minister would announce a reduction in the number of liquor shops on 15 August. Without saying a word about the protests, the Chief Minister issued a statement warning of stern action against those who engaged in violence at TASMAC shops. The Chief Minister must declare the government's position on the liquor trade run by the government itself. If the government deliberately delays TASMAC announcements so that issuing them close to the election will help reap votes, it would be gravely wrong. Once something is known to be harmful, it must be abandoned that very moment. Let evil depart at once. Let good begin at once.”
That was the detailed editorial written by *Ananda Vikatan*.

With the 2016 general election arriving in May, it is now reported that the AIADMK, continuing its habitual practice of deception in everything, plans to announce in its election manifesto that it will introduce prohibition if returned to power. It hopes, with deceitful intent, to fool the people in the election and reap their votes! What it had no heart to do during five years in office, it now proposes to promise in a manifesto to deceive the country!

If, even when the Central government has not implemented prohibition across India, the AIADMK announces in its election manifesto that it will introduce prohibition on returning to power, it can only be a “sleight-of-hand” announcement intended to deceive ordinary people.

Reports now emerging bear out the *Ananda Vikatan* editorial. With the 2016 general election arriving in May, it is reported that the AIADMK, continuing its habitual practice of deception in everything, plans to announce in its election manifesto that it will introduce prohibition if returned to power. It hopes, with deceitful intent, to fool the people in the election and reap their votes! What it had no heart to do during five years in office, it now proposes to promise in a manifesto to deceive the country!

During the debate on the Governor's Address in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly this January, “Midas” Minister Natham Viswanathan said: “We say that we will support prohibition if the Central government brings it throughout India.” When DMK Legislative Party leader M. K. Stalin earnestly asked that prohibition at least be introduced gradually, following the neighbouring states, Minister Natham Viswanathan rejected even that. But now that the election is approaching, if they announce in their manifesto that they will introduce prohibition on returning to power even though the Centre has not introduced it throughout India, it can only be a “sleight-of-hand” announcement intended to deceive ordinary people.
With affection,
M. K.
16 March 2016
